1999-02-01.title: British and Irish Leaders Try to Calm Fears Over Ulster Peace
1999-02-01.abstract: Irish and British leadership and highest-ranking officials in Northern Ireland move to allay fears that peace effort in Ulster is crumbling; effort has been stalemated over dispute between Roman Catholic and Protestant leaders on issue of disarming overwhelmingly Catholic Irish Republican Army; Seamus Mallon, Deputy First Minister of Northern Ireland Assembly, calls for compromise retreat from brinkmanship that could collapse peace effort (M)

1999-02-01.title: Malaysia's Leader Rattles Meeting of Financial Elite
1999-02-01.abstract: Prime Min Mahathir Mohamad of Malaysia, nemesis of world's financial leaders in recent years, rattles participants at annual retreat of World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland; is been by turns charming and combative; his favorite notion--of controlling capitalism's excesses--has been subtext of half of seminars being held at Davos (M)

1999-02-01.title: Mixed Reception to Jordanian Understudy
1999-02-01.abstract: Jordanians react with tentative optimism to King Hussein's appointment of son, Crown Prince Abdullah, as heir apparent, replacing brother, Crown Prince Hassan; many feel that while challenges facing country are fewer than when father was crowned King in 1952, they still require extraordinary deftness (M)

1999-02-01.title: Gallery Thieves Get $2 Million In Mexican Master's Paintings
1999-02-01.abstract: Thieves, threatening onlookers with firearms, raid prestigious Lopez Quiroga art gallery in Mexico City and steal 12 paintings by Rafael Tamayo, one of country's great masters; value of stolen works, belonging to Mexican and American collectors, is estimated at $2 million (M)

1999-02-01.title: Annan Fears Backlash Over Global Crisis
1999-02-01.abstract: UN Sec Gen Kofi Annan, in address at World Economic Forum in Davos, Switzerland, warns of backlash against global economy; challenges big business to enter unusual and formal compact with UN to promote human rights, labor and environmental practices in countries where they do business; photo (M)

1999-02-02.title: 'Question Mark' to Become Venezuela's President Today
1999-02-02.abstract: Economic and international policies of Venezuela's Pres-elect Hugo Chavez remain mystery on eve of February 2 inauguration, despite efforts to polish image with visits to Europe, Latin America and US since election in December 1998; photo (M)

1999-02-02.title: World Briefing
1999-02-02.abstract: AFRICA    GUINEA-BISSAU: THOUSANDS FLEE FIGHTING  Thousands of people scrambled to flee Bissau, the capital, as fighting intensified between loyalist and rebel forces, and the rebels announced ''a final assault'' on the city. At least 15 people were reported killed. The fighting appeared to signal the end of an accord in November that halted a five-month civil war in the former Portuguese colony.   (AP)

1999-02-02.title: Tony Blair Rides Triumphant, Either as a Visionary or as a Good Promoter
1999-02-02.abstract: Britain's Prime Min Tony Blair, who has large majority in Parliament and ineffective opposition, presses 10-year project to revitalize Britain and make it Europe's leader but many Britons are raising questions; even though he is most popular Prime Minister in British history with wide public appeal, his Government has been criticized for engaging mostly in presentation, or 'spin,' while lacking real substance; photo (M)

1999-02-02.title: Milosevic Advantage: In Talking Peace He Can Win Big
1999-02-02.abstract: News analysis says threats by Clinton Administration and its NATO allies to bomb Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic into ceding effective control over Kosovo Province not only make it easier for him to justify eventual concessions but also make him indispensable to any settlement (M)

1999-02-02.title: Orthodox Confront U.S. Reform Rabbis at Western Wall
1999-02-02.abstract: 100 strictly Orthodox yeshiva students, in ugly confrontation, surround group of American Reform rabbis seeking to pray at Western Wall, Jerusalem, booing loudly and hurling insults past officers from border police; clash is latest skirmish in war between Orthodox and Reform and Conservative movements, which many fundamentalists believe to be such dilution of Judaism that they are not same religion at all; photo (M)

1999-02-03.title: So Paris Begins a Campaign to Strengthen Multilateral Institutions : Going It Alone, U.S. Upsets France
1999-02-03.abstract: PARIS—  France is undertaking an active campaign to strengthen multilateral institutions as part of an effort to define the United States' potential for unilateral action as one of the world's great worries. It is, in effect, an attempt to limit American power and to convince other countries that they should work together to contain it.

1999-02-03.title: New Chief to Battle Venezuela's 'Cancer'
1999-02-03.abstract: Hugo Chavez is inaugurated as President of Venezuela, and promises peaceful revolution to remove 'cancer' of corruption; photo; emotional two-hour address describes country 'rotting to the core' as 'social time bomb' of hunger, disease and malnutrition ticks away; reassures business leaders and foreign investors by promising to attack budget deficit, support regional currency and use military for antipoverty efforts such as road-building; calls for referendum to rewrite Constitution to remove judiciary from politics, add checks and balances and remove one-term limit for president; Fidel Castro attends, taking copious notes and embracing Chavez afterwards (S)

1999-02-03.title: Israelis Debate Burying Bronfman Among Heroes
1999-02-03.abstract: Edgar Bronfman Sr's reported wish to be buried amid Israel's great heroes prompts World Zionist Organization to consider whether Canadian billionaire, 69, might become first non-Israeli to be buried in Mount Herzl cemetery; Bronfman, leader in world Jewish causes, is quoted by biographer Michael Shashar as saying burial in Mount Herzl would prove he was 'real mensch'; his photo (S)

1999-02-03.title: In Japan, From a Lifetime Job to No Job at All
1999-02-03.abstract: Japanese accustomed to lifetime employment are shocked by layoffs stemming from deep economic woes; Japan's 4.1 percent unemployment rate strikes blow to country's self-image and to social contract that has kept public complacent and one party in power for decades; jobless battle shame and despair, as well as challenge of finding work, and humiliation is even flaring into rare public anger; Takaharu Akimoto recalls disbelief at being asked to resign, seven years short of retirement; he is among older, higher-paid workers with particular problems finding new jobs; photo; graph; Government is going to great lengths to keep jobless figures from rising, providing generous subsidies to companies to keep workers (M)

1999-02-03.title: Kaduna Journal; If Army Can't Play Politics, There's Always Polo
1999-02-03.abstract: Nigeria's military, which has ruled Africa's most populous nation for many years, suffers setbacks that have unsettled many old soldiers who live in Kaduna; photos of polo club, British institution introduced in 1904; map (Kaduna Journal) (S)

1999-02-04.title: New President in Venezuela Proposes to Rewrite the Constitution
1999-02-04.abstract: Newly-installed Pres Hugo Chavez Frias sends tremors through political establishment by moving to rewrite Venezuelan Constitution; process would likely replace newly elected Congress with one that has more of his allies; Chavez and his supporters see 1961 Constitution as symbol of corrupt political system that has fed popular resentment and apathy; it was written to keep Marxist groups then aligned with Cuban-backed guerrillas out of potlical process; coalition that helped elect Chavez contains Marxist and socialist groups who sorely want to replace that document (M)

1999-02-04.title: Space-Based Mirror May Light Skies Tonight
1999-02-04.abstract: Astronauts aboard Russian space station Mir will attempt to bounce sunlight off 82-foot mirror and direct rays toward ground, producing beams up to 10 times brighter than full moon; astronomers are protesting experiment, saying such developments threaten to produce new kinds of light pollution that could blind telescopes exploring night sky; diagrams (M)

1999-02-04.title: London Journal; A Right Honorable Briton? Hardly, It Turns Out
1999-02-04.abstract: British commentators are mining Shakespeare and Greek tragedies to come up with parallels for fall of Jonathan Aitken, high-born former Cabinet minister who has admitted committing perjury during libel case; his marriage and his fortune are gone, and his likely future is in prison; photo (M)

1999-02-04.title: Legislators In Brazil Are Leaving Ruling Party
1999-02-04.abstract: Pres Fernando Henrique Cardoso of Brazil may face more fractious second term as raft of newly elected members of Congress switch parties just before taking office; some deputies leave ruling party to join smaller party in which they could hold more sway; they promise more critical stance twward Cardoso's proposals; several legislators have left Cardoso's party for party of Itamar Franco, Governor of their state, who is in middle of battle of nerves with Cardoso regarding his state's debt to Federal Government (M)

1999-02-04.title: John Service, a Purged 'China Hand,' Dies at 89
1999-02-04.abstract: John S Service, first of China experts purged from State Department in McCarthy era, dies at age 89; photos (M)

1999-02-05.title: To Paris, U.S. Looks Like a 'Hyperpower'
1999-02-05.abstract: PARIS—  Foreign Minister Hubert Vedrine says that he now defines the United States as a "hyperpower," a new term that he thinks best describes "a country that is dominant or predominant in all categories."

1999-02-05.title: India Gives Rushdie a Visa to Return, and Muslims Quickly Warn of Protests
1999-02-05.abstract: Salman Rushdie is granted visa to return to India, his native land; country's banning of his novel The Satanic Verses in 1988 began chain of events that led to death threats by offended Muslims and a life in hiding; decision to allow Rushdie to return is made in highest reaches of Indian Government, but various Muslim leaders protest and make veiled threats about his safety if he does return (M)

1999-02-05.title: Average Malaysians Ask:'What Crisis?'
1999-02-05.abstract: ALOR GAJAH, Malaysia—  It has been 18 months since Haris Aziz and his family began hearing the words "economic crisis" and by now one might have expected them to be feeling the pinch.

1999-02-05.title: World Briefing
1999-02-05.abstract: EUROPE    BRITAIN: PINOCHET HEARINGS END -- The House of Lords concluded 12 days of hearings to determine whether Gen. Augusto Pinochet, the former Chilean dictator, is immune from arrest and extradition to Spain on charges of torture, kidnapping and murder. Lord Browne-Wilkinson, chairman of the seven-member Law Lords, Britain's highest court, gave no clear indication of when a decision would be announced.   Alan Cowell (NYT)    RUSSIA: INQUIRY ON TYCOON WIDENS -- An investigation of the financier Boris A. Berezovsky has been widened to include Aeroflot, in which he had a stake. Those being questioned include the former Defense Minister Yevgeny Shaposhnikov; when he was head of Aeroflot, he directed the airline to channel hard-currency earnings to a Swiss company half-owned by Mr. Berezovsky, the newspaper Moskovsky Komsomolets reported.   Celestine Bohlen (NYT)

1999-02-05.title: Austria Banks' Plan On Holocaust Funds
1999-02-05.abstract: American lawyer Edward Fagan says two Austrian banks he represents are circulating proposal that could settle Holocaust-era claims against them; banks are Bank Austria and Creditanstalt (S)

1999-02-06.title: Board Ousts BMW Chief and His Heir
1999-02-06.abstract: FRANKFURT—  - The maker of BMW autos announced the departures of its two top executives Friday in a rare German boardroom revolt that analysts said was triggered by heavy losses at the company's British Rover auto-making subsidiary.

1999-02-06.title: In Islam, Brain Death Ends Life Support
1999-02-06.abstract: Muslim religion sanctions use of life support to keep a patient alive as long as there is some hope of revival, but once death is inevitable the faithful believe that the family should accept God's will and not keep organs functioning artificially (S)

1999-02-06.title: News Analysis; Old Order Changes
1999-02-06.abstract: Changing of the guard across that Arab world is heralded by the impending death of King Hussein of Jordan; most Arab rulers are aging, and several are ill, and in some places the path of succession remains uncertain (M)

1999-02-06.title: 4 I.R.A. Gang Members Sentenced in Killing of Irish Detective
1999-02-06.abstract: Four members of an Irish Republican Army gang are sentenced in Dublin to terms of 11 to 14 years for killing Gerry McCabe, an Irish detective, during robbery of a bank van in Adare, Ireland, in 1996; outrage is voiced in many quarters over decision by prosecutors to drop charges from murder and manslaughter, which they did because crucial witnesses had been intimidated and refused to repeat in court what they had told police (M)

1999-02-06.title: Istanbul Journal; A New Bridge? Not in My Backyard!
1999-02-06.abstract: Reports that Turkish Government plans a third bridge over Bosporus sets off outpouring of civic protest in Istanbul's Arnavutkoy neighborhood, which has evidently been chosen as European terminus for the bridge; some opponents of the bridge say it would not ease traffic congestion, for which they blame poor access roads, and they are pushing for a rail tunnel under the Bosporus instead; photo; map (M)

1999-02-07.title: With German Craft Rules, It's Hard Just to Get to Work
1999-02-07.abstract: Article on countless numbers of skilled Germans caught up in broad political battle over job protection and workplace flexibility in nation with some of strictest work rules in Europe, which have helped produce one of best-paid and most productive work forces in world; map (M)

1999-02-07.title: 6 Years After Crash, Talk of Cover-Up
1999-02-07.abstract: Dutch Parliament is investigating crash of El Al cargo plane into Amsterdam housing complex that killed 43 people on ground in Oct 1992; over 1,100 rescue workers and residents say they were somehow contaminated by burning aircraft, or its mysterious cargo; El Al in Oct 1998 revealed that cargo included vats of chemicals that can be used in manufacture of deadly nerve gas Sarin; investigation is expected to hear some 70 witnesses and last months more, drawing together reports of illness and allegations of cover-up by El Al and Dutch Government that have inflamed public passions in Amsterdam; photo (M)

1999-02-07.title: New Leader's First Test Lies in Internal Politics
1999-02-07.abstract: Crown Prince Abdullah of Jordan, appointed acting monarch by Jordanian Cabinet, faces as his first test internal political challenges rather than foreign affair crises; few analysts expect major political problems in first months of rule, but say he will be forced to move in short order to replace some members of current Cabinet, including unpopular Prime Min Fayez Tarawneh (M)

1999-02-07.title: King's Nemesis Is an Unyielding Cancer
1999-02-07.abstract: Non-Hodgkin's lymphoma, cancer from which King Hussein of Jordan lies near death, comes in many forms; while prognosis varies significantly according to type, any form can act unpredictably (M)

1999-02-07.title: Kosovo Talks Begin With Stern Warning
1999-02-07.abstract: The Kosovo peace conference opened to exhortations from President Jacques Chirac of France that both sides put aside their differences. The start of the talks was delayed by Serbian maneuvering and the subsequent late arrival of the ethnic Albanian delegation.

1999-02-08.title: Easing Tensions, Disney Gains O.K. to Show 'Mulan' in China
1999-02-08.abstract: China, easing tensions with Walt Disney Co over Disney's backing of Kundun, film sympathetic to Dalai Lama, will allow theatrical release in China of film Mulan; Disney's efforts to regain China's favor noted (M)

1999-02-08.title: Murder in Mexico: Reformers Uncover Police Plot
1999-02-08.abstract: Mexico City reform government probe of Nov death in Mexico City of Frederick Mc Phail Jr has led to arrest and indictment of five police officers; authorities say officers forced him to drink entire bottle of liquor after they stole his bank card and looted his account; have identified dozen other people, including three foreign tourists, allegedly victimized by same rogue officers; Mc Phail's father, Frederick Sr, stirred public outrage over slaying with newspaper advertisements; he calls case example of rampant corruption and police violence, interview; photo (M)

1999-02-08.title: Ailing Yeltsin May Go to Jordan To Show He Is Still in Command
1999-02-08.abstract: Ailing Pres Boris N Yeltsin signals he is still in command with plan to lead Russian delegation to funeral of Jordan's King Hussein; trip would be less demanding than diplomatic mission, give Yelsin chance to be seen with world leaders and let him upstage Prime Min Yevgeny M Primakov (M)

1999-02-08.title: Hussein of Jordan, Voice for Peace, Dies
1999-02-08.abstract: Jordan's King Hussein, who has ruled Hashemite Kingdom since 1952, dies of cancer at age 63; his eldest son, Abdullah, 37, is crowned King; appears on television to console grieving nation; pledges to maintain father's legacy; names half-brother Hamzeh, 18, Crown Prince in gesture that should solidify ranks within royal family; Pres Clinton and other heads of state around world pay tribute to Hussein; Clinton and wife, Hillary, former Presidents Gerald Ford, Jimmy Carter and George Bush, British Prime Min Tony Blair, host of Arab leaders, including Palestinian leader Yasir Arafat, and Israeli delegation led by Pres Ezer Weizman and also including Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu and former Prime Mins Shimon Peres and Yitzhak Shamir will attend funeral; map; photos (M)

1999-02-08.title: Tullamore Journal; Travelers' Tale: Irish Nomads Make Little Headway
1999-02-08.abstract: Ireland's 40,000 nomads, formerly disparagingly called tinkers, are now politely called travelers; most have given up horsecarts and covered wagons of their ancestors for car-towed trailers; still have hard time finding regular jobs or adequate campsites; 2,000 live in illegal camps that lack normal facilities; Government has ordered local councils to provide adequate facilities within next five years; map; photo (M)

1999-02-09.title: World Briefing
1999-02-09.abstract: AFRICA    ETHIOPIA, ERITREA: CONFLICT WIDENS -- The border conflict between the two countries on the Horn of Africa widened to a second front, as Ethiopian warplanes attacked an Eritrean position near Tsorona. It was the first time that either side had used planes in battle since they agreed to halt air strikes in June. After an eight-month cease-fire, the two nations resumed fighting over the weekend.   Ian Fisher (NYT)

1999-02-09.title: Jordanians Grieve Over the Passing of a King, and Worry About the Future
1999-02-09.abstract: Thousands of grieving Jordanians throng funeral route in Amman to pay final respects to King Hussein; break through barricades into streets; scene described; photo (M)

1999-02-09.title: Marius Schoon, 61, Is Dead; Foe of Apartheid Lost Family
1999-02-09.abstract: Marius Schoon, opponent of apartheid who had been fighting amnesty application by police spy who killed Schoon's wife and daughter with mail bomb, dies at age 61; photo (M)

1999-02-09.title: Lochinver Journal; At Long Last, Scots Can Lord It Over the Lairds
1999-02-09.abstract: Donald Dewar, Britain's Secretary for Scotland, says first order of business when new Scottish Parliament convenes in May 1999 will be to reform ancient land-holding structure, under which half of Scottish land in private hands belongs to just 350 people, and 1,500 private estates account for 80 percent of country; under plans, local communities would gain right to buy estates at market value and be able to tap National Lottery money to help raise purchase price; purchase of 13-hamlets near Lochinvar from Swedish owner by residents, who set up Assynt Crofters' Trust cooperative self-government discussed; photos; map (M)

1999-02-09.title: Leftist Opposition Party in Mexico Wins Two Governors' Races
1999-02-09.abstract: Left-of-center Party of the Democratic Revolution wins gubernatorial election in Baja California Sur and powerful office of Mayor in Mexico City; victories demonstrate continuing growth of party that has capitalized on dissension in governing Institutional Revolutionary Party (M)

1999-02-10.title: Germans Approve New Plan To Pay Holocaust Victims
1999-02-10.abstract: The German Government said today that it had agreed in principle to establish a  compensation fund, financed by the biggest names in German industry and banking, for victims of Nazi horrors.    The proposal, announced here today by a top aide to Germany's new chancellor, Gerhard Schroder, appears to be an effort to short-circuit a wave of lawsuits in American courts against German companies that made use of slave labor and forced labor during World War II, or profited from assets seized from Jews and other victims.

1999-02-10.title: Japan Holds Executive in Sale Of Sensitive Machines to China
1999-02-10.abstract: A former executive of a manufacturer of precision instruments has been arrested in Japan for reportedly flouting laws that ban the sale to Communist countries of certain types of equipment that could be used to develop nuclear weapons.     The suspect, Isao Sasaki, who retired last year as senior managing director at Ryokosha K.K., an affiliate of the electronics giant Hitachi Ltd., was arrested on Saturday and charged with responsibility for selling telecomparators to China by way of South Korea. Telecomparators are measuring devices that are accurate to less than two-thousandths of a millimeter.

1999-02-10.title: F.B.I. Helped Chile Search For Leftists, Files Show
1999-02-10.abstract: The F.B.I. tried to track suspected associates of Chilean leftists in the United States in the 1970's on behalf of the Government of Gen. Augusto Pinochet, the Chilean dictator, newly declassified documents show.    The Federal Bureau of Investigation sent agents in New York and Dallas to search for two people whose names were found in the address book of a courier for an underground Marxist group in Chile. The courier was arrested in Paraguay in 1975, and the Paraguayan police passed on the information to American officials, the records show.

1999-02-10.title: World Briefing
1999-02-10.abstract: ASIA    INDIA: STATE LEADER QUITS -- The Chief Minister of Orissa State has submitted a letter of resignation, grudgingly taking ''moral responsibility'' for a recent spate of violence, including the killing of an Australian missionary and his two sons by Hindu militants. The Minister, Jayanti Ballabh Patnaik, gave the letter to his Congress Party leader, Sonia Gandhi.   Barry Bearak (NYT)

1999-02-10.title: Jordanians Pay Tribute to Late King and His Heir
1999-02-10.abstract: Thousands of Jordanians from desert camps, urban shacks and sleek modern villas made their way to the royal palace today to offer sympathy to King Abdullah and prayers for his late father, King Hussein.    Queen Noor, the widow of King Hussein, and other women in the royal family prayed separately for the first time at the King's grave.

1999-02-11.title: Tough Controls Blamed for More Deaths on Border
1999-02-11.abstract: American Civil Liberties Union and other American and Mexican human rights groups complain to Organization of American States that newly erected border fences and more vigorous enforcement by Border Patrol has spurred illegal immigrants to take dangerous routes to California through mountain passes and deserts, spurring surge in deaths; say Mexican diplomats have documented deaths of 360 immigrants along California border since 1994, when United States began crackdown (M)

1999-02-11.title: Indonesia Ship Sinks; 280 Feared Drowned
1999-02-11.abstract: Port officials say at least 280 people are feared to have drowned when ship, Harta Rimba, sank last weekend between the Indonesian islands of Borneo and Sumatra; say 19 people were rescued (S)

1999-02-11.title: Trickiest Divides Are Among Big Powers at Kosovo Talks
1999-02-11.abstract: Hosts of Kosovo peace talks, Rambouillet, France, focus on their divisions; France and United States agree to compromise under which United Nations Security Council will be asked to 'endorse,' not 'authorize' deployment of NATO peacekeepers; more practical debate is over how quickly ethnic Albanian's Kosovo Liberation Army should be required to demobilize (M)

1999-02-11.title: Immigration Ruling by Hong Kong Court Is Generating New Tensions With Beijing
1999-02-11.abstract: Hong Kong court ruling that children born in mainland China to parents who are Hong Kong residents have right to live in Hong Kong stirs tensions between China and Hong Kong; judges used case to make broad statements about independence of Hong Kong's judiciary and about Hong Kong's autonomy in general; Tung Chee-hwa, Hong Kong's chief executive, says he will send Justice Secretary to Beijing to discuss conflict; photo (M)

1999-02-11.title: Judge Gags Press at Trial of Malaysia's Former Deputy Premier
1999-02-11.abstract: Judge in corruption trial of Malaysia's ousted Deputy Prime Minister, Anwar Ibrahim, bars news media from publishing Anwar's court statements about Prime Min Mahathir Mohamad and Police Chief Abdul Rahim Noor; has rejected Anwar's charges of political conspiracy (M)

1999-02-12.title: Plans for Rival Rallies on Religion Issue Stir Fears in Israel
1999-02-12.abstract: An escalating conflict between religious and secular Jews is expected to spill into the streets here on Sunday when a mass prayer rally against the liberal Supreme Court faces a counter-rally by nonreligious Israelis who feel that democracy is under attack.    Political leaders worked unsuccessfully today to persuade religious leaders to cancel the prayer rally, which some feared could turn violent. By police prediction, at least 100,000 demonstrators are expected, a loose coalition against ''the judicial dictatorship'' including ultra-Orthodox Jews, religious Zionists and Jewish settlers from the West Bank.

1999-02-12.title: In Bid to Court China, Pope Weighs Visit To Hong Kong
1999-02-12.abstract: Roman Catholic officials in Asia and Rome say that Pope John Paul II is considering a visit to Hong Kong later this year; such a visit could soften decades-old standoff between Vatican and China's Communist Government, which has sovereignty over Hong Kong (M)

1999-02-12.title: Israelis Disclose '88 P.L.O. Talks
1999-02-12.abstract: The newspaper Haaretz reported today that Yitzhak Rabin and Shimon Peres held secret talks with the P.L.O. in 1988, well before the 1993 Oslo accord, when they were in the Government of Yitzhak Shamir, the rightist Prime Minister from the Likud Party.     The unattributed account, apparently leaked by a participant in the talks, in Paris, said that Mr. Rabin and Mr. Peres negotiated when they were Defense and Foreign Ministers in Mr. Shamir's unity Government, composed of the Likud and Labor Parties.

1999-02-12.title: MOVIE GUIDE : La Nina de Tus Ojos
Gideon Rafael, 85, a Founder of the Israeli Foreign Ministry
1999-02-12.abstract: Gideon Rafael, a founder of the Israeli Foreign Ministry and one of its most accomplished and longest-serving diplomats, died on Wednesday in Jerusalem. He was 85.    In a career that began before Israel was established in 1948, Mr. Rafael negotiated with the Nazis to send Jews to Palestine, served at the United Nations and in Europe and conducted secret contacts with Arab countries.

1999-02-13.title: Terni Journal; Politics and Heartbreak In St. Valentine's Home
1999-02-13.abstract: Fall of Terni, Italy, city government forces cancellation of most of Valentine celebrations in depressed mill town whose key attraction is shrine of St Valentine; city believes its patron saint, one of 17 Roman Catholic St Valentines, could have spawned modern and highly lucrative Valentine's Day cult; map; photo (M)

1999-02-13.title: New Iraq Panel Has Veterans of Arms Dispute
1999-02-13.abstract: United Nations panel created to review Iraq's disarmament record and recommend ways of persuading Pres Saddam Hussein to honor commitment to abandon unconventional weapons is dominated by arms control experts from UN Special Commission whose inspections angered Iraq; Amb Celso L N Amorim of Brazil, who heads panel and two others, on humanitarian affairs, prisoners of war and Kuwaiti property, announces members of all three; arms panel does not include Special Commission head Richard Butler, but includes his deputy, Charles Duelfer; Amorim concedes Iraq has not yet agreed to cooperate with reviews; United States Amb Peter Burleigh lauds panel, interview (M)

1999-02-13.title: Albright Due at Kosovo Talks to Push Pacts on Forces
1999-02-13.abstract: Clinton Administration says State Sec Madeleine K Albright will visit Kosovo peace conference, Rambouillet, France, to push delegates to forge military accords to end fighting; says she will propose internal security arrangements, including cut in Serbian troops and police forces, dissolution of ethnic Albanian rebel army and creation of new Kosovo police force; Serbian Pres Milan Milutinovic visits Serbian delegates; warns of guerrilla war if NATO bombs Serbia; photo (M)

1999-02-13.title: Waves From Brazilian Economic Crisis Pound a Uruguayan Beach Resort
1999-02-13.abstract: Punta del Este, upscale Uruguayan beach resort, is feeling impact of Brazil's economic crisis; Brazilian tourists are cancelling and Argentinians cutting back on stays; some Argentinians are switching to Brazilian beach resorts, lured by Brazilian currency devaluation; city's elite fears slump will speed shift away from dependence on long summer rentals by Argentinians; weighs bid for American tourists; map (M)

1999-02-13.title: World Briefing
1999-02-13.abstract: ASIA    CHINA: BID TO HEAL RIFT -- Hong Kong's Secretary of Justice, Elsie Leung, arrived in Beijing to try to work out a constitutional dispute with China over a recent court decision granting mainland residents with a parent in Hong Kong the free right of immigration. She warned that a quick solution was unlikely.   Elisabeth Rosenthal (NYT)    INDIA: STATE GOVERNMENT DISMISSED -- The Government won the President's permission to dismiss the government of the state of Bihar and impose central rule. The decision was prompted by a second round of killings of low-caste, landless people in just over two weeks by a private army sponsored by landlords in Bihar.   Celia Dugger (NYT)

1999-02-14.title: On Russia's Far East Fringe, Unrealpolitik
1999-02-14.abstract: Politics takes a turn toward farce in Vladivostok, far eastern Russian port, which has become an object lesson in how hard it can be to raise a democratic society out of ruins of Soviet empire; a power struggle is being waged by autocratic Governor of Primoriye region, Yevgeny Nazdratenko, and Viktor Cherepkov, the highly eccentric mayor of Vladivostok until he was ousted from office in December; Cherepkov claims extrasensory perception; Nazdratenko has received a mysterious $1 million prize for leadership from World Aristocratic Academy, an organization whose only known address is a post office box in the Bahamas; photos; map (M)

1999-02-14.title: Clinton on Kosovo: 'We Can Make a Difference''
1999-02-14.abstract: Transcript of Pres Clinton's radio address on US commitment to send troops to Kosovo

1999-02-14.title: Malaysia Says Its Much-Criticized Financial Strategy Has Worked
1999-02-14.abstract: Mayalsian Central Bank briefs foreign fund managers on nation's investment outlook, saying its decision last September to impose strict controls on foreign investment have stablilized the country and put it on the road to recovery; asserts with delight that the disaster predicted by Internatinal Monetary Fund, US Treasury Department and other critics of capital controls have not occurred; says rules have since been relaxed and Malaysia is now ready for business; map; photo (M)

1999-02-14.title: Unabashed Politician's Statements Jeopardize Irish Government
1999-02-14.abstract: Ireland's coalition Government faces possible collapse after Padraig Flynn, Ireland's member of European Commission, goes on television, jokes about how hard it is maintain his three houses on $150,000 a year and brushes aside questions about the $75,000 that he allegedly received from a Dublin entrepreneur in 1989; members of Parliament from Prime Min Bertie Ahern's Fianna Fail party approve resolution demanding that Flynn explain the affair (M)

1999-02-14.title: Mexico Opponents See Politics in Clinton Visit
1999-02-14.abstract: Pres Clinton is not scheduled to meet with any opposition figures during visit to Mexico, and they charge that he is taking sides before presidential election in 2000 (M)

1999-02-15.title: For-Profit Colleges Divide Malaysians
1999-02-15.abstract: SUBANG JAYA, Malaysia—  If it weren't for the students wandering the halls with books under their arms, one might be forgiven for thinking Taylor's College was the headquarters of a medium-sized corporation.

1999-02-15.title: Poland Seeks Role in Fund for Nazi Forced Laborers
1999-02-15.abstract: Poland is seeking central role in shaping financial fund being set up by German companies to compensate those who were forced laborers during World War II and other victims of Nazi horrors; Wieslaw Walendziak, chief of staff to Prime Min Jerzy Buzek, says approximately two million Poles were deported to Germany after Nazis invaded in 1939 and subsequently worked under prison-like conditions in German companies or on farms; estimates 520,000 currently live in Poland (S)

1999-02-15.title: Doubts on Kosovo
1999-02-15.abstract: News analysis: while Pres Clinton's proposal to send American troops to Kosovo has heartened promoters of more active American role in preventing ethnic carnage, its has also aroused fears that Administration is marching into ancient swamp without clear idea how not to get mired in it; while stopping bloodshed is worthy objective in itself, doubts focus on whether Kosovo really touches on American national interests and whether Administration really knows what it is getting into, or how to get out of region notorious for instability and complexity (M)

1999-02-15.title: In Britain, Schools Repackage Softer Image
1999-02-15.abstract: LONDON—  For generations, parents have sent their children to British boarding schools for an experience often described as "toughening up." But economic times and social attitudes have changed. These days, cold dormitories and colder showers are more likely to put off, rather than attract, prospective parents — not to mention their progeny. And the schools themselves have had to learn a hard lesson: adapt or die.

1999-02-15.title: Ruling Party In Mexico Wins Election For Governor
1999-02-15.abstract: Election authorities in Mexico's Guerrero State declare Rene Juarez Cisneros, candidate of governing Institutional Revolutionary Party, winner of gubernatorial election; opposition angrily contests results, alleging irregularities in vote count (M)

1999-02-16.title: Kosovo Peace Plan Takes Shape As Russia Prods Serbs on Troops
1999-02-16.abstract: Broad shape of agreement to end ethnic conflict in Serbian province of Kosovo emerges, with Russia playing key role at talks in Rambouillet, France, to persuade Serbs to allow NATO peacekeepers on their territory; French Pres Jacques Chirac says Russians, traditional ally of Serbs, feel ground force in Kosovo is best solution to stopping bloodshed in separatist province and that Russian soldiers are likely to be part of deployment; photo (M)

1999-02-16.title: Ruling on Tight Jeans and Rape Sets Off Anger in Italy
1999-02-16.abstract: Ruling by Italian high court, that woman cannot be raped while wearing jeans since tightness makes them impossible to remove without her help, reopens angry national debate about rape and about how judges view sexual assault; group of female lawmakers is on open-ended 'jeans strike,' vowing to wear jeans to Parliament until decision is changed (M)

1999-02-16.title: Nigerian Ex-Ruler Captures Lead in Race for President
1999-02-16.abstract: Gen Olsegun Obasanjo, who earned international esteem by becoming Nigeria's first military ruler to give up power voluntary, wins nomination of country's leading political party as its presidential candidate; move sets stage for his return to position he held 20 years ago, but as democratically elected President; he vows to make Nigeria great again; photo (M)

1999-02-16.title: World Briefing
1999-02-16.abstract: ASIA    HONG KONG: DEPORTATIONS BLOCKED -- A Hong Kong court has blocked the deportation of 18 mainland Chinese who contend that they have the right to residency under a landmark ruling by the territory's Court of Final Appeal. The decision may further inflame relations with China, which has said the ruling is a mistake that must be ''rectified.'' A Hong Kong spokesman said Beijing and Hong Kong had ''narrowed'' their areas of disagreement.    Mark Landler (NYT)

1999-02-16.title: French Chief Will Press Plan on Iraq With Clinton
1999-02-16.abstract: French Pres Jacques Chirac, at upcoming meeting with Pres Clinton in Washington, will press Clinton on France's proposal to lift oil embargo against Iraq if it allows resumption of international inspections of its weapons programs (S)

1999-02-17.title: TOP KURD'S ARREST UNLEASHES RIOTING ALL ACROSS EUROPE
1999-02-17.abstract: Protestors enraged by capture of Abdullah Ocalan, foremost Kurd rebel leader, storm diplomatic posts throughout Europe and elsewhere, taking hostages and setting fires; Kurds in Paris, London, Moscow, Frankfurt, Milan, Bern and more than dozen cities as far away as Sydney, Australia, stage violent protests; seize consulates; battle police; threaten mass suicides; hundreds are arrested; most of approximately two dozen hostages are freed; at least three Kurds are seriously injured after setting themselves on fire; focus of anger is against Greece, which temporarily provided Ocalan shelter, and Kenya where he was captured; both countries are believed to have betrayed him in end; Kurds demonstrate in Kurdish neighborhood in Istanbul, setting cars on fire; demonstrations of joy erupt elswhere in Turkey, where many people view Ocalan as terrorist; photos (M)

1999-02-17.title: Northern Ireland Assembly Approves Political Restructuring
1999-02-17.abstract: Northern Ireland Assembly approves sweeping changes in political structure in predominantly Protestant British province, aimed at giving Roman Catholics minority more power and ending sectarian violence that has killed more than 3,200 people in last 30 years; continuing dispute over disarmament of Irish Republican Army threatens to delay formation of Cabinet, which is supposed to be completed by March 10 (M)

1999-02-17.title: In Snatching a Fugitive Rebel, Ankara Wins Opportunities on Several Fronts
1999-02-17.abstract: Kurdish guerrilla leader Abdullah Ocalan, Turkey's most-wanted fugitive whose army has been fighting for Kurdish homeland, is captured in Kenya and flown back to Turkey; Ocalan, whose 14-year war has cost lives of estimated 30,000 people, has been sought by Turkish authorities for more than decade; could face execution; his semiclandestine existence since 1980 and details of his capture discussed (M)

1999-02-17.title: Kurds' Rebel Leader May Prove a Discredit to His Cause
1999-02-17.abstract: Rise of Abdullah Ocalan as revolutionary leader of Kurds may have been been one of greatest modern tragedies to befall hapless group, which has been exploited and slaughtered by dozens of regimes over centuries; Ocalan, whose return to Turkey as prisoner seems to have ended his 14-year career as guerrilla leader, is by most accounts dogmatic and tyrannical; even though many Kurds have followed him, with many still considering him their leader, in eyes of world he has done as much as anyone to discredit their cause; many with whom he formed Kurdistan Workers Party in 1970's were later killed, some tortured to death on his orders; others fled to live clandestinely in Europe after he branded them as traitors for challeging him or pressing for more democracy within revolutionary movement; photo (M)

1999-02-17.title: Qunu Journal; Mandela's Childhood Left a Fond Spot in His Heart
1999-02-17.abstract: Article profiles Qunu, South Africa, childhood hometown of Pres Nelson Mandela; photo; maps (M)

1999-02-18.title: World Ills Are Obvious, the Cures Much Less So
1999-02-18.abstract: Fourth and final article in series, Global Contagion: A Narrative, on global financial crisis; crisis that began 19 months ago in Thailand is knocking on gates of American heartland and could be landmark crisis of post-cold-war era; while simplest challenge is for US to sustain strong growth rates, broader task will be to prevent nationalistic catacylsms in worst-off countries, like Russia and Indonesia, and to contain political and security risks of explosive frustration if crisis bites further into places like China and Latin America; while economy has demonstrated tremendous flexibility and resilience, uncertainties arise because US stock market is 64 percent higher than in December 1996 when Alan Greenspan, Federal Reserve chairman, warned against irrational exuberance; photos; chart (L)

1999-02-18.title: East Timor Seems Suddenly to Be on Verge of Independence
1999-02-18.abstract: Clinton Administration and foreign diplomats welcome Indonesian Pres B J Habibie's statements favoring independence for East Timor, but fear that if break is too sudden it could lead to chaos and even civil war as Timorese factions maneuver for power; maps (M)

1999-02-18.title: World Briefing
1999-02-18.abstract: EUROPE    RUSSIA: UKRAINE TREATY RATIFIED -- The Upper House of Parliament voted to ratify a treaty that effectively abandons the Kremlin's claims on Crimea on condition that Ukraine's legislature guarantee that the Russian fleet can maintain its existing fleet there. Prime Minister Yevgeny M. Primakov had sought the treaty in part to brake Ukraine's plans to join NATO.    Michael Wines (NYT)

1999-02-18.title: Israel Denies Role but Fears Reprisal for Ties to Turkey
1999-02-18.abstract: Israel braces for reprisals after killings of three Kurdish protesters at its consulate in Berlin; Israel was already target of Kurdish rage because of its strong links with Turkey, and followers of Abdullah Ocalan have accused Israeli intelligence service Mossad of helping track down the Kurdish rebel leader (M)

1999-02-18.title: Among Rebels' Officer-Trainees, No Sign Kosovo Fighting Is Over
1999-02-18.abstract: Officer-trainees in Kosovo Liberation Army are still preparing for war even as talks progress in France to reach agreement between rebels in Kosovo province and Serbian Govt; those at training school near village of Suva Rika say they will obey superiors' commands following any peace with Serbs; say core of rebel force must remain intact and armed, in order to protect ethnic Albanians when Western forces leave; over period of year, rebel army has gone from ragtag guerrilla bands to force of between 10,000 and 15,000, equipped with increasingly sophisticated weapons and bent on achieving not only autonomy for Kosovo being offered at peace talks, but independence for Kosovo's ethnic Albanian majority; photos; map (M)

1999-02-19.title: Pressures Mount Against Defiant Milosevic
1999-02-19.abstract: Military and diplomatic pressure mounts on Pres Slobodan Milosevic to choose between tolerating NATO-led peacekeepers in Kosovo or suffering NATO air strikes for refusing them; Secretary of State Madeleine K Albright says she again warned Milosevic and that she will return to talks in Rambouillet, France; Milosevic has remained in Belgrade, and diplomats do not rule out high-level trip there before final agreement is reached; Clinton Administration and European officials expect Milosevic to conclude finally that air strikes would be more destabilizing to his rule than NATO-led ground troops keeping peace in Kosovo (M)

1999-02-19.title: Neighbors Rally to Jordan, Easing Financial Fears
1999-02-19.abstract: King Abdullah's new regime in Jordan is gaining its financial footing, helped by strong and somewhat unexpected show of support from his neighbors; country has received promises of financial support from Saudi Arabia and Israel and pledges of continued trade ties from Syria and Iraq; this is in addition to assurance of long-term backing already provided by Clinton Administration and International Monetary Fund; Jordanians remain concerned about prospects for growth in stagnant local economy; there have been no reports of significant capital flight, and Amman's banks and stock market and currency traders opened their doors after official week of mourning for King Hussein; photo (M)

1999-02-19.title: At a Genocide Trial, French Is a Handicap
1999-02-19.abstract: Former Rwandan mayor, Jean Paul Akayesu, is only man ever convicted of genocide; Akayesu wants to appeal genocide verdict handed down last fall by hiring criminal lawyer from Montreal, but his request has been quashed by administrators at United Nations tribunal in Tanzania that is trying suspects in 1994 killing of over half million Rwandans; ban, based on fact that there are already 9 Canadian defense lawyers, baffles some legal scholars; they contend policy is arbitrary and could jeopardize credibility of UN tribunal (M)

1999-02-19.title: 3 Greek Officials Removed Over Rebel Kurd's Capture
1999-02-19.abstract: Prime Min Costas Simitis of Greece replaces his Foreign Minister and two other Cabinet members in effort to contain growing scandal over Greece's role in capture of Kurdish rebel leader, Abdullah Ocalan; Greek Government officials give their explanation of how Greece came to secretly harbor Ocalan, and then was unable to protect him from Turkish security forces; enraged Kurdish protesters have occupied Greek diplomatic posts all over world to protest what they see as Greece's betrayal of Kurdish leader; many Greeks express skepticism about Government's explanation, suspecting that Ocalan was sold out (M)

1999-02-19.title: San Luis Potosi Journal; Mexico's Troubadors Turn From Amor to Drugs
1999-02-19.abstract: Hundreds of Mexican country bands sing lyrics that chronicle drug traffickers' daily lives and violent routines; extraordinary popularity of their music in Mexico and in United States underscores profound roots drug industry has sunk into North American popular culture, suggesting that millions of fans quietly admire smugglers' fabled wealth, anti-establishment bravura and bold entreprenuerial skills; photo of popular band singing in San Luis Potosi about a rooster, parrot and goat, code words for drugs; map (M)

1999-02-20.title: The President Describes 'the Challenge in Kosovo'
1999-02-20.abstract: Excerpts from Pres Clinton's statement on Kosovo at joint news conference with French Pres Jacques Chirac, and from their answers to questions

1999-02-20.title: Libya Asks Annan About Pan Am Trial Rules
1999-02-20.abstract: Libya asks United Nations Sec Gen Kofi Annan to clarify assurances that he has given to try to persuade Libya to turn over suspects in bombing of Pan American flight over Scotland in 1988 (M)

1999-02-20.title: Stretching Federal Labor Law Far Into South Pacific
1999-02-20.abstract: Federal officials say American companies in Saipan exploit garment workers who are overwhelmingly immigrants from China; lawyers filed class-action suit in Jan accusing garment makers and major retailers of underpaying and mistreating workers; many Chinese workers say they came to Saipan voluntarily to earn about five times what they made in China; say they can save $6,000 to $10,000 in two years and return home quite well off; graph; map; photos (M)

1999-02-20.title: Serbs Yield No Ground; Envoys Seem Resigned
1999-02-20.abstract: Clinton Administration seems resigned to not having agreement by Feb 20 deadline in Kosovo peace talks; officials are hopeful that ethnic Albanian delegation will sign settlement; Yugoslav Pres Slobodan Milosevic refuses to see chief US negotiator, Christopher R Hill (M)

1999-02-20.title: World Briefing
1999-02-20.abstract: EUROPE  BRITAIN: DEBT RELIEF URGED -- Chancellor of the Exchequer Gordon Brown said he would use a meeting in Germany of the Group of Seven to reinforce American and German calls for accelerated debt relief for the world's poorest nations. Mr. Brown said he would press for $50 billion worth of debt to be wiped out.    Alan Cowell (NYT)

1999-02-21.title: In Serb Capital, More Ennui Than Anguish Over a Raid
1999-02-21.abstract: Few in Belgrade expect NATO to bomb Serbia in dispute over Kosovo province; feel that West is being unfair to Serbia; see Pres Slobodan Milosevic finding way to avert attack (M)

1999-02-21.title: Turks vs. Kurds: Turning Point?
1999-02-21.abstract: Analysis of impact on Kurdish guerrilla movement in Turkey of capture and apparent taming of rebel leader Abdullah Ocalan; much will depend on whether Turkey's leaders can shift unyielding stand and allow emergence of moderate Kurdish groups; map; photo (M)l

1999-02-21.title: Major Powers Agree to Take Modest Steps On Economy
1999-02-21.abstract: Group of Seven industrial nations finance ministers, meeting, Bonn, agree to create modest forum of top regulators, who will meet twice a year to consult and seek ways to improve quality of financial information; reject demands by German and French leaders for broad new 'architecture' aimed at taming violent financial markets swings (M)

1999-02-21.title: Olivetti Makes $60 Billion Bid for Telecom Italia, a Major Rival
1999-02-21.abstract: Olivetti SpA opens drive to acquire Telecom Italia SpA, far larger rival, for equivalent of $60.56 billion; Telecom Italia prepares to battle hostile bid (M)

1999-02-21.title: In Filmmaker's Ideal Russia, a Presidential Role?
1999-02-21.abstract: Interview with Nikita S Mikhalkov, director of film The Barber of Siberia, who says he will weigh running for president himself if he cannot find candidate to support; possible candidacy reflects disillusionment Russians have with choices among infirm Pres Boris N Yeltsin and slew of pretenders widely seen as power-hungry opportunists; he calls for restoration of real or idealized virtues of Czarist Russia; photo (M)

1999-02-22.title: Lauder Enters Into Deal With Yugoslav Company
1999-02-22.abstract: RSL Communications, company headed by cosmetics heir Ronald S Lauder, signs contract with Telekom Serbia to provide long-distance telephone service to and from Yugoslavia in defiance of US policy prohibiting investment in Serbia; RSL denies doing anything improper or violating US sanctions, even though US Treasury Dept says deal was not approved and is under active review (M)

1999-02-22.title: Bombs-to-Plowshares Program Criticized
1999-02-22.abstract: Report by General Accounting Office criticizes key part of Clinton Administration's effort to stop spread of nuclear, biological and chemical weapons by helping employ scientists from former Soviet Union on peaceful projects; study of two Energy Department programs concludes that American aid may have instead supported scientists still working in Russia on projects related to weapons of mass destruction; says some scientists or their institutes may also have provided expertise not only to weapons programs in Russia but also to those of other countries (M)

1999-02-22.title: India Promises, With Pakistan, To Seek Peace
1999-02-22.abstract: Prime Min Atal Behari Vajpayee of India and Prime Min Nawaz Sharif of Pakistan hold joint news conference in Lahore and issue joint declaration pledging immediate steps to reduce risk of nuclear war and to seek solutions to half a century of raging disputes, such as conflict over Kashmir; memorandum of understanding pledges to build trust in each other through series of high-level bilateral meetings and to adopt measures to prevent any accidental launching of nuclear weaponry; pledges to continue declared moratoriums on further nuclear trials and agrees to announce in advance any testing of ballistic missiles; Vajpayee extends invitation for Sharif and wife to visit New Delhi (M)

1999-02-22.title: Saipan Journal; Palm Trees and Sun (and Who Needs an Address?)
1999-02-22.abstract: Growth of Saipan Island, US Commonwealth in Northern Mariana Islands, creates some impatience with absence of street names and numbers; legislature passed bill calling for action, but job remains incomplete; many islanders say street names and numbers would be unnecessary bother and unwelcome intrusion of modern life; maps; photo (M)

1999-02-22.title: Narrow Edge in Nigerian Legislative Races
1999-02-22.abstract: Nigeria's main party, People's Democratic Party, still clings to lead in legislative elections despite weakening of support in eastern section of country; presidential election is just one week away; among winners in legislative contests is Lola Abiola-Edewor, daughter of late opposition figure Moshood K O Abiola; photo; map (M)

1999-02-23.title: Serbian Police Reportedly Beat Two Cease-Fire Monitors
1999-02-23.abstract: Serbian police beat and harass cease-fire monitors in Kosovo Province as reports emerge of attacks on villages and fighting between Serbian troops and ethnic Albanian rebels; troops reportedly destroy homes of ethnic Albanians in villages near Vucitrn, 18 miles northeast of provincial capital of Pristina; 4,000 people reportedly flee; rebels reportedly attack Serbian village in area, killing one person and wounding two others; Organization for Security and Cooperation in Europe, which provides monitors to police shaky cease-fire, lodges strong protest with Serbian Govt; map; photo (M)

1999-02-23.title: Subcontinent Sees a Light
1999-02-23.abstract: News analysis of implications of visit by India's Prime Min Atal Behari Vajpayee to Pakistan, where he held summit with Prime Min Nawaz Sharif (M)

1999-02-23.title: Turkey and Greece Trade Words Over Kurd
1999-02-23.abstract: Turkey and Greece trade harsh words as crisis over captured Kurdish guerrilla commander, Abdullah Ocalan, further sours already tense relations; Suleyman Demirel, Turkey's President, says Greece should be added to list of countries that support terrorism and harbor terrorists for allegedly protecting Ocalan after he fled to Kenya, where he was captured by Turkish commandos; Greece accuses Turkey of trying to use it as scapegoat for Kurdish problem, which it urges Turkey to solve quickly; photo (M)

1999-02-23.title: Governing Party in Mexico Wins 2 State Votes
1999-02-23.abstract: Mexico's governing Institutional Revolutionary party wins decisive victories in elections for governor in states of Quintana Roo and Hidalgo; victories underscore party's resilience and point to vulnerability of divided opposition (S)

1999-02-23.title: British Report Finds Racism Pervades Police
1999-02-23.abstract: Long-awaited British Govt report says London's police force is riven with pernicious and institutionalized racism and suggests that its chief officers accept that conclusion as condition of keeping their commands; makes 70 recommendatons for changes in police practices to usher in fundamental transformation of race relations in Britain (M)

1999-02-24.title: U.S. and China Trying Hard For Breakthrough on Trade
1999-02-24.abstract: United States and China are making crash effort to resolve issues that block China from joining World Trade Organization; Deputy Treas Sec Lawrence H Summers comments on effort after meeting with Prime Min Zhu Rongji, Beijing; United States wants accord that will lower China's tariffs and commit it to legally enforceable schedule for opening markets (M)

1999-02-24.title: World News Briefs
1999-02-24.abstract: ASIA    INDIA: BIHAR TAKEOVER IN DOUBT -- The Government's decision to dismiss the state government of Bihar, scene of two recent massacres of low-caste rural laborers, and to impose central rule appears doomed in Parliament. The opposition Congress Party has decided to vote against ratification of central rule, saying the massacres are insufficient reason to dismiss an elected government.   Hari Kumar (NYT)    UZBEKISTAN: 30 HELD IN FATAL BOMBINGS -- President Islam Karimov, left, said 30 people, described as mostly radical Islamists who underwent training in Chechnya, Afghanistan and Tajikistan, had been held on charges of organizing bombings last week that he called an attempt to assassinate him. The blasts, in Tashkent, the capital, killed 16 people.   (Reuters)

1999-02-24.title: 100 Million Restless Chinese Go Far From Home for Jobs
1999-02-24.abstract: China is on move, with estimated 10 percent of population migrating, mostly in search of better jobs; rural migrants, with strong emotional ties to villages and unable to get urban residence permits needed for entirely legal life in cities, mostly seek to earn money to take home; most of migrants, now thought to number 100 million, return to hometowns every Chinese New Year, in what may be largest seasonal migration in world; their presence is transforming urban life, mixing together China's distinct regional cultures and giving rise to new rootless urban underclass; map; photos (M)

1999-02-24.title: In Reversal, Cuban Exiles Will Be Tried In Puerto Rico
1999-02-24.abstract: Federal Judge Hector M Laffitte, reversing his earlier ruling to move trial of seven Cuban exiles charged with plotting to kill Fidel Castro to Miami, orders proceedings to continue in Puerto Rico; warns case could still be moved to Miami if prosecutors fail to establish Puerto Rico as proper venue at trial; defendants are Angel Alfonso-Aleman, Angel Hernandez Rojo, Juan Bautista Marquez, Francisco Secundino-Cordova, Jose Antonio Llama, Jose Rodriguez and Alfredo Domingo Otero (M)

1999-02-24.title: Guben Journal; Hatred, Born of a Hitlerian Mix, on March Again
1999-02-24.abstract: Case of Algerian Farid Guendoul, 28, who bled to death fleeing neo-Nazi skinheads, Guben, has shocked Germany, spurring appeals for fight against xenophobia and renewed questions about nation's immigration policy; rise of racism in depressed area of former east German underscores resistance Chancellor Gerhard Schroder faces in trying to ease citizenship requirements; map; photo (M)

1999-02-25.title: Crash of a Chinese Jetliner Kills More Than 60
1999-02-25.abstract: China Southwest Airlines plane, Russian-made jet, crashes on descent to Wenzhou, killing all 61 people aboard and people on ground; crash will likely raise questions about safety of Russian planes, which have been involved in relatively high proportion of Chinese air disasters even though they make up tiny franction of commercial fleet (M)

1999-02-25.title: How U.S. Hit a Snag In the Kosovo Talks
1999-02-25.abstract: The Clinton Administration appears to have walked into the Kosovo peace talks without a clear idea of what to do if the ethnic Albanians failed to follow the script.    The Administration expected the Kosovo Albanians to accept NATO troops as their protectors, allowing it then to press the Serbian leader to sign an accord. But it didn't turn out that way as the Kosovo Albanians, who want independence from the Serbs, balked at a deal for autonomy.

1999-02-25.title: Ukrainian Seeks U.S. Asylum
1999-02-25.abstract: Former Ukraine Prime Min Pavlo Lazarenko, who was detained as he tried to enter United States, seeks political asylum; faces corruption charges in Ukraine and claims he fears persecution by Pres Leonid Kuchma's Government (S)

1999-02-25.title: Olivetti Races To Add Funds For a Heftier Telecom Bid
1999-02-25.abstract: ROME—  Olivetti SpA, fighting fiercely to keep alive its takeover bid for Telecom Italia SpA, was scrambling Wednesday to raise about 23 trillion to 25 trillion lire ($13.5 billion to $14.7 billion) in additional funding, financial sources in Milan said.

1999-02-25.title: D.E.A. Chief Warns Senate On Traffickers In Mexico
1999-02-25.abstract: Thomas A Constantine, Drug Enforcement Administration head, warns Mexican drug trafficking organizations pose unprecedented criminal threat to United States, Senate Caucus on International Narcotics Control; cites their access to large staffs, arms arsenals and best technology; sidesteps question about whether Mexico deserves to be certified as cooperating in drug-fighting efforts (M)

1999-02-26.title: GUATEMALAN ARMY WAGED 'GENOCIDE,' NEW REPORT FINDS
1999-02-26.abstract: Independent truth commission report concludes that United States gave money and training to Guatemalan military that committed 'acts of genocide' against Mayans during Guatemala's 36-year brutal civil war; report contradicts years of official denials of torture, kidnapping and executions of thousands of civilians in war that commission estimates killed more than 200,000 people; commission's nine-volume report lists Central Intelligence Agency training of Guatemalan officer corps in counterinsurgency techniques as key factor that had significant bearing on human rights violations during armed confrontation; staff member of commission says panel also found evidence that United States had knowledge of genocide and still supported Guatemalan military; commission was set up as part of United Nations-supervised peace accord that ended war in 1996; photo (M)

1999-02-26.title: Struggling Ukraine Teeters Between East and West
1999-02-26.abstract: Pres Leonid Kuchma has failed to overhaul Ukraine's Soviet-era economy, and its Parliament is swayed by current and former Communists who block land privatization and overhaul of crippling tax system; as result, Ukraine continues to teeter between East and West; it has trappings of Western democracy all in place, but bureaucracy still shows its Soviet genealogy; its basketcase economy remains lashed to its Russian past; fiscal experts say country will be hard pressed to pay its foreign debt this year; in October, Ukraine will hold its third presidential election since independence in 1991; pro-European incumbent, Kuchma, who has been unable to raise living standards, may be defeated by candidate from Socialist and Communist left, most of whom favor closer ties with Russia; photos; map; chart (L)

1999-02-26.title: Milosevic Is Warned Against Launching Offensive : NATO Troops Move Closer To Kosovo to Counter Serbs
1999-02-26.abstract: PARIS—  NATO moved forces closer to Kosovo on Thursday after U.S. intelligence reported that Serbian troops, backed by scores of tanks, were massing just across the border.

1999-02-26.title: Palestinians Reject Netanyahu Offer as Campaign Tactic
1999-02-26.abstract: Palestinian officials reportedly rebuffed Prime Min Benjamin Netanyahu's quiet offer to carry out several of Israel's obligations under frozen peace agreement signed last fall; Netanyahu, through intermediary, proposed that Israel open up promised southern safe-passage route between Palestinian territories in West Bank and Gaza, and he volunteered to release some Palestinian petty criminals from Israeli jails; but Palestinians did not take initiative seriously, because Netanyahu made no offer to cede any additional territory in West Bank; Saeb Erekat, senior Palestinian peace negotiator, says he views Netanyahu's offer as domestic political gesture to help him get re-elected; photo (M)

1999-02-26.title: U.S. Seeks to End 'Mixed Messages' Over Lockerbie Suspects
1999-02-26.abstract: Clinton Administration asks Secretary General Kofi Annan to handle all communications with Libya about turnover of two suspects wanted in 1988 bombing of Pan American flight in Lockerbie, Scotland; also asks Annan to discourage efforts by envoys from Saudi Arabia and South Africa to give Libya further assurances about trial, treatment of suspects or sanctions if men are handed over; decision to narrow mediation effort reflects growing concern that Col Muammar el-Qaddafi might be receiving 'mixed messages,' in form of assurances that neither United States nor Britain would agree to (M)

1999-02-27.title: A Warning At the U.N. To Libyans
1999-02-27.abstract: United States and Britain warn at United Nations Security Council that they will seek added sanctions against Libya unless it surrenders suspects in 1988 Lockerbie airplane bombing within 30 days to be tried before Scottish court in Netherlands; Sec Gen Kofi Annan backs deadline (M)

1999-02-27.title: Counting Nigerians Before They Vote
1999-02-27.abstract: Olusegun Obasanjo, retired general who is frontrunner in Nigeria's Presidential race, portrays self as only safe bridge in transition to democracy; he and rival, Olu Falae, wind up short campaigns that offer no clear picture of their ideology or plans to deal with economic, political and social problems; map; photo (M)

1999-02-27.title: Searing Indictment
1999-02-27.abstract: Guatemalan truth commission report blaming military for deaths of nearly 200,000 people in 36-year civil war surprised nearly everyone with bluntness and severity of its judgments and recommendations; weakens political standing of military high command and their civilian allies and leaves them open to prosecution; charge of genocide against groups of Mayan people is not covered by amnesty they were granted in 1996 (M)

1999-02-27.title: World Briefing
1999-02-27.abstract: MIDDLE EAST    IRAN: ELECTION TURNOUT IS HEAVY -- Iran's first local elections in 20 years drew a heavy turnout in a contest that could give supporters of President Mohammad Khatami the upper hand in a power struggle against hard-liners in the Islamic Government. Polling stations in some towns ran out of ballots and voting hours in larger cities had to be extended. (AP)

1999-02-27.title: In 'Clarification' on Immigrants, Hong Kong Court Bows to China
1999-02-27.abstract: Hong Kong's highest court bows to intense pressure and responds to Hong Kong government's request for 'clarification' with extraordinary statement that it had not meant to challenge Chinese authority with its recent ruling granting residency to mainland children of Hong Kong parents; does not alter ruling; lawyers and democracy advocates score stand (M)

1999-02-28.title: Regulators Give Green Light to Olivetti's Bid for Telecom Italia
1999-02-28.abstract: A $60 billion bid by the No. 2 Italian phone company, Olivetti S.p.A., for Telecom Italia was given a green light to move forward today, when Italian stock market regulators ruled that it was legally admissible.    The ruling came two days after Telecom Italia's chairman, Franco Bernabe, won boardroom backing for what is expected to be a long and bitter corporate battle, unaccustomed for the Italian business world and reminiscent of big American hostile buyouts of the 1980's.

1999-02-28.title: Peacekeeping in Kosovo: Grave Test for NATO Allies
1999-02-28.abstract: With Serbian troops apparently disregarding a NATO ultimatum to stop fighting in Kosovo and withdraw from the southern Serbian province or face air attacks, the allies could face the gravest test of their credibility since they forced a settlement of the war in Bosnia more than three years ago.    Time and again, the allies have been insisting since last spring that they would not stand by and watch, as they did for years in Bosnia, while Serbian forces committed atrocities against civilians. Yet not even the threat of 400 bombers, including B-52's, has been able to get the Yugoslav President, Slobodan Milosevic, to agree to accept a force of 28,000 international peacekeepers for Kosovo as part of a settlement that would provide autonomy for the province.

1999-02-28.title: Feminist Propels Outcry at Brutal Mexico Killings
1999-02-28.abstract: Within hours after youths passing by a Juarez drainage canal on a recent day discovered the body of a 13-year-old girl, the most recent victim in a long series of sexual killings here, Esther Chavez Cano was rushing to spread the dismal news.    She was on the phone to members of the feminist organization she heads. She was typing E-mail messages to congresswomen and journalists. And in news interviews, Ms. Chavez, a 65-year-old former accountant for Kraft Foods, was accusing the new Governor of Chihuahua of breaking campaign promises made last year to investigate the killings of women in Juarez more seriously than his predecessor did.

1999-02-28.title: New Delhi Bid To Take Over Eastern State Hits Obstacles
1999-02-28.abstract: For Prime Minister Atal Behari Vajpayee, the euphoria of his historic peacemaking visit to Pakistan was short-lived.     The stories about high-stakes nuclear diplomacy were quickly bumped off the front pages this week by his Government's mad scramble to salvage its move to dismiss the government of the eastern state of Bihar, one of India's poorest and most populous.

1999-02-28.title: End Fighting, U.N. Council Tells Ethiopia And Eritrea
1999-02-28.abstract: The United Nations Security Council today demanded an immediate halt to the fighting between Ethiopia and Eritrea, after Ethiopian forces apparently smashed through the defenses along its disputed 600-mile border with Eritrea and appeared to be pushing deeper into the country in the direction of the Red Sea.     At an emergency meeting here, the Council called on both sides to refrain from further hostilities and resolve their border dispute on the basis of the peace plan drawn up the Organization for African Unity.
